comment: Both ontologies contain a class labeled User with closely matching intended meaning as a person participating in the system.
Exact lexical match on Person; both denote human participants.
Exact lexical match on Document; both represent document-like entities in the conference domain.
Exact lexical match on Review; both are subclasses of Document.
Exact lexical match on Paper; both are subclasses of Document and represent submitted scholarly works.
Exact lexical match on Conference; both model a conference event.
Exact lexical match on Administrator; both denote privileged system users administering conference processes.
Exact lexical match on Author; both represent users who author papers, though local constraints differ.
COCUS Abstract is a subclass of Paper, and CMT PaperAbstract is also a paper subtype; labels are lexically similar but not identical.
Both classes denote the full version of a paper; naming differs but semantics are very close.
COCUS Committee is a user role-like class, while CMT ProgramCommittee is a committee entity. They are related in conference governance but not clearly equivalent.
COCUS models reviewer as a role, while CMT models Reviewer as a person class. They correspond conceptually but are ontologically different kinds.
COCUS Author_Role is a role class; CMT Author is a person/user class. The concepts are related but not equivalent.
COCUS Admin_Role is a role executed by an administrator, whereas CMT Administrator is a user class.
COCUS Event is broader and has subclasses such as Conference, Workshop, and Symposium. CMT Conference corresponds to one specific kind of event.
Workshop and Conference are both scholarly event types, but not equivalent; related only at a general event level.
Symposium and Conference are related scholarly event types but are not interchangeable.
COCUS Corresponding_Author is explicitly a subclass of Author; CMT Author is the nearest broader author class.
An approval email in COCUS is related to a positive decision outcome, approximated in CMT by Acceptance, but one is a document and the other a decision.
A rejection email communicates a rejection decision; these are related but ontologically distinct.
Both properties capture submission behavior. COCUS submit links User to Document, while CMT submitPaper links Author to Paper, making CMT more specific.
Both properties express positive administrative decisions, but one targets Event and the other Paper.
Both indicate provenance/agent relation to a created artifact, but domains and semantics differ substantially.
